summary_text: The New Beautiful Me is a feature-rich AI-powered tarot reading app with impressive breadth: readings with 4 spread types and 4 interpretation styles, daily cards with streaks, rich journaling with mood tracking and image uploads, a 78-card library with personal notes, deep analytics, learning paths, custom spreads, weekly recaps, badges, and sharing. The frontend UX is polished with Framer Motion animations and a cohesive dark mystical theme. Critical gaps are in authentication (no password reset, no email verification), backend robustness (no rate limiting, no error monitoring, monolithic 1055-line Lambda), and testing coverage (no component tests, no API tests). Growth potential is strong with clear paths for streaming AI, push notifications, and community features.

findings.title: No password reset / forgot password flow
findings.description: There is no forgot-password or password-reset mechanism anywhere in the application. The login page only has email/password fields with no recovery link. The API Lambda has changePassword (requires current password) but no reset-via-email flow. Users who forget their password are permanently locked out of their account and all their data.
findings.impact: Users permanently lose access to their accounts. This is the single biggest product risk — it will drive churn and generate support requests with no resolution path.
findings.recommendation: Implement a full password reset flow: forgot-password link on login page, API endpoint to generate a time-limited reset token, SES email delivery with reset link, and a reset-password page that validates the token and sets a new password.

findings.title: No email verification on registration
findings.description: User registration accepts any email address without verification. The register endpoint in the Lambda creates accounts immediately with no confirmation step. This allows fake/typo emails, making password reset impossible even after PROD-001 is fixed, and opens the door to spam account creation.
findings.impact: Unverified emails undermine account recovery, enable spam registrations, and reduce trust in user data quality.
findings.recommendation: Add email verification: send a confirmation link via SES on registration, store a verified flag in DynamoDB, and gate full account access on verification completion.

findings.title: Navigation bar overflows on mobile with 10+ links
findings.description: The Navigation component renders 10 route links (Home, Daily, Reading, Journal, History, Library, Insights, Recap, Spreads, Learn) plus theme toggle, settings, and logout in a horizontal scrollable row. On mobile screens this creates a poor experience with excessive horizontal scrolling and no clear visual hierarchy.
findings.impact: Mobile users (likely the majority audience for a personal tarot app) struggle to find navigation items, reducing feature discovery and engagement.
findings.recommendation: Implement a hamburger menu or bottom tab bar for mobile. Group secondary items (Library, Insights, Recap, Spreads, Learn) under a 'More' menu. Consider a bottom navigation pattern with 4-5 primary actions.

findings.title: No component-level tests — only regression/integration tests
findings.description: The test suite has 6 files but they are all data tests (tarot-cards.test.ts, storage.test.ts) or regression tests generated by previous audits (ADA, compliance, QA, SEO). There are zero component tests for any React component, page, or user flow. Critical flows like reading, journal entry, and authentication have no test coverage.
findings.impact: Refactoring or adding features risks breaking existing functionality with no safety net. Bugs in critical flows (auth, reading, payment-adjacent features) won't be caught before deployment.
findings.recommendation: Add component tests for critical paths: login/register flow, reading flow (spread select → draw → interpret), journal create/edit, daily card draw. Use Vitest + Testing Library (already in devDependencies). Aim for 60%+ coverage on src/app/ pages.

findings.title: No production monitoring or alerting
findings.description: There is no monitoring, alerting, or observability setup for the production environment. Lambda functions have no CloudWatch alarms, no error tracking (Sentry or similar), no uptime monitoring, and no performance dashboards. API errors are only visible if someone manually checks CloudWatch logs.
findings.impact: Production issues (Lambda errors, DynamoDB throttling, API Gateway 5xx spikes) go undetected until users report them. AI interpretation failures are silently swallowed.
findings.recommendation: Add CloudWatch alarms for Lambda error rates and duration, API Gateway 4xx/5xx rates, and DynamoDB throttle events. Set up SNS notifications for alerts. Add Sentry for client-side error tracking.

findings.title: No pagination on history and journal pages
findings.description: Both the history page and journal page load all readings/journals at once via getReadings() and getJournals(). The API also returns all items without pagination support. As users accumulate hundreds of readings, page load times will degrade significantly.
findings.impact: Performance degradation over time. Heavy users will experience slow page loads and high memory usage, potentially causing the app to feel sluggish or crash on mobile devices.
findings.recommendation: Add cursor-based pagination to the API (DynamoDB ExclusiveStartKey) and implement infinite scroll or paginated views on the frontend. Start with 20-item pages.

findings.title: No auto-save on journal entries
findings.description: The journal entry editor has no auto-save or draft mechanism. If a user accidentally navigates away, closes the browser, or experiences a crash while writing a long reflection, all content is lost. The only save mechanism is the explicit 'Save Entry' button.
findings.impact: Users lose in-progress journal entries, which is especially painful for long reflections. This creates frustration and may discourage detailed journaling.
findings.recommendation: Implement auto-save to localStorage every 30 seconds with a visual indicator. Restore drafts on page load. Clear draft on successful save.

findings.title: No filtering or sorting on reading history page
findings.description: The history page shows all readings in reverse chronological order with no ability to filter by spread type, date range, or favorites, and no sorting options. The compare mode exists but finding specific readings to compare requires scrolling through the entire list.
findings.impact: As reading history grows, finding specific readings becomes increasingly difficult. Users can't quickly surface Celtic Cross readings or filter to a specific month.
findings.recommendation: Add filter chips for spread type and favorites. Add date range picker. Add sort options (date, spread type). These filters already have data available in the Reading type.

findings.title: Learning path progress stored only in localStorage
findings.description: The Learn page tracks lesson completion progress using localStorage ('tarot-learn-completed' key). This means progress is lost when clearing browser data, switching devices, or using a different browser. All other user data is synced to DynamoDB.
findings.impact: Users lose learning progress when switching devices or clearing browser data, creating an inconsistent experience compared to other features that persist server-side.
findings.recommendation: Add a user preferences/progress table or attribute in DynamoDB. Sync learn completion state to the backend. Fall back to localStorage for unauthenticated users.

findings.title: Hardcoded domain URLs in share features
findings.description: The reading page share functionality hardcodes 'thenewbeautifulme.com' in the share URL construction and Open Graph share image generation. This makes the codebase environment-dependent and breaks sharing in development/staging environments.
findings.impact: Cannot test sharing in non-production environments. Any domain change requires finding and updating all hardcoded references.
findings.recommendation: Extract the base URL to an environment variable (NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL). Reference it in share URLs, OG image generation, and any other domain-dependent code.

findings.title: Native alert() used instead of Toast system for confirmations
findings.description: The settings page uses window.confirm() for delete account confirmation. The app has a ToastProvider in the layout but some flows still use native browser dialogs which break the visual design language.
findings.impact: Minor UX inconsistency. Native dialogs look out of place in the polished dark-themed UI.
findings.recommendation: Replace window.confirm() with a custom confirmation modal component that matches the app's design system.

findings.title: No API documentation for Lambda endpoints
findings.description: The Lambda API has 20+ endpoints but no API documentation — no OpenAPI spec, no endpoint reference, no request/response examples. The only documentation is inline code comments.
findings.impact: Slows down development velocity. New contributors must read the Lambda source to understand available endpoints, expected payloads, and error responses.
findings.recommendation: Create an OpenAPI 3.0 spec documenting all endpoints, authentication requirements, request/response schemas, and error codes.

findings.title: No rate limiting on AI interpretation endpoints
findings.description: The AI interpretation Lambda has no rate limiting. Any authenticated user can make unlimited calls to all 5 AI modes (interpret, followup, patterns, compare, recap). Each call invokes the Anthropic Claude API which has per-token costs.
findings.impact: A single user (or automated abuse) could generate significant Anthropic API costs. No protection against accidental or intentional overuse.
findings.recommendation: Implement rate limiting at the API Gateway level (usage plans with throttling) or in the Lambda with a DynamoDB counter. Suggested limits: 20 interpretations/day, 50 follow-ups/day per user.

findings.title: JWT tokens lack refresh mechanism — 30-day expiry with no renewal
findings.description: JWT tokens are issued with a 30-day expiry. There is no refresh token mechanism. When the token expires, users are silently logged out and must re-enter credentials. The auth context checks token validity on load but has no way to renew an expiring token.
findings.impact: Users experience unexpected logouts every 30 days with no warning. This is especially jarring for daily users who may lose their streak or be confused by sudden auth loss.
findings.recommendation: Implement a refresh token flow: issue a short-lived access token (1 hour) with a long-lived refresh token (90 days). Add a token refresh endpoint and client-side auto-refresh logic.

findings.title: Capacitor mobile config exists but native app is not built
findings.description: A capacitor.config.ts file exists with appId 'com.thenewbeautifulme.app' and webDir 'out', but there are no iOS/Android platform directories, no native plugins configured, and no mobile build scripts. The config suggests mobile was planned but never implemented.
findings.impact: The Capacitor config creates a false impression that mobile builds are supported. It's dead code that may confuse contributors.
findings.recommendation: Either commit to building the mobile app (add platforms, configure native plugins for haptics/notifications, set up app store builds) or remove the Capacitor config to reduce confusion. The PWA already provides mobile web access.

findings.title: Library page accessible without auth but note-saving fails silently
findings.description: The Library page does not use AuthGuard, so unauthenticated users can browse the card library. However, the card notes feature calls saveCardNote() which requires authentication. If an unauthenticated user tries to save a note, the API call will fail with a 401 but the UI doesn't clearly communicate this.
findings.impact: Unauthenticated users may try to save notes and be confused when they don't persist.
findings.recommendation: Either wrap the notes section in an auth check (show a 'Sign in to save notes' prompt) or add AuthGuard to the entire Library page for consistency with other pages.

findings.title: No delete confirmation on custom spreads
findings.description: The spreads page allows deleting custom spreads with a single click on the trash icon — no confirmation dialog, no undo mechanism. The history page has undo-delete via toast but spreads deletion is immediate and permanent.
findings.impact: Users can accidentally delete a carefully designed spread with one mis-tap, especially on mobile. No way to recover.
findings.recommendation: Add either a confirmation dialog before deletion or an undo-via-toast pattern matching the history page implementation.

findings.title: No delete confirmation on journal entries
findings.description: In the journal list page, the delete action on diary entries calls the API immediately without asking for confirmation. While the history page has an undo-based delete pattern (toast with 5.5s delay), the journal page does not implement this pattern.
findings.impact: Users can accidentally delete journal reflections with a single tap. Journal entries contain personal reflections that cannot be recreated.
findings.recommendation: Add either a confirmation dialog or adopt the same undo-toast pattern used in history/page.tsx for consistent delete UX across the app.

findings.title: Monolithic Lambda handler at 1055 lines
findings.description: The entire API is a single Lambda function (lambda/api/index.mjs) handling all routes: auth, readings, journals, daily cards, spreads, uploads, stats, sharing, export, and account management. This makes the code hard to navigate, test, and deploy incrementally.
findings.impact: Any code change requires redeploying the entire API. Testing individual routes is difficult. New developers face a steep learning curve navigating a 1055-line file.
findings.recommendation: Refactor into separate modules per domain (auth, readings, journals, etc.) imported by the main handler. Consider splitting into separate Lambda functions per API domain for independent scaling and deployment.

findings.title: No API/Lambda integration tests
findings.description: The test suite contains frontend regression tests and unit tests for tarot cards and storage, but there are zero tests for the Lambda API handler. Auth flows, CRUD operations, authorization, and error handling are all untested at the API level.
findings.impact: API changes risk breaking existing functionality with no automated verification. Auth bugs, data corruption, or authorization bypasses could ship undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add integration tests for the Lambda handler. Mock DynamoDB with @aws-sdk/lib-dynamodb mocks or DynamoDB Local. Test auth flows, CRUD operations, error cases, and authorization boundaries.

findings.title: No staging environment
findings.description: The Terraform configuration defines a single production environment. There is no staging or preview environment for testing changes before they reach users.
findings.impact: All changes go directly to production. No safe way to test infrastructure changes, API updates, or data migrations before they affect real users.
findings.recommendation: Add a staging workspace to Terraform (or use Terraform workspaces) with a separate DynamoDB table, Lambda, and CloudFront distribution. Consider Vercel preview deployments for the frontend.

findings.title: Inconsistent loading state patterns across pages
findings.description: Some pages use PageSkeleton component (spreads, journal), others show no loading indicator, and some use simple text. The loading experience varies across the app.
findings.impact: Inconsistent UX during page loads. Some pages feel polished while others appear to lag or flicker.
findings.recommendation: Standardize loading states: use PageSkeleton consistently for initial page loads, add shimmer/skeleton components for card grids and lists.

findings.title: No offline status indicator for PWA
findings.description: The app is a PWA with a service worker, but there is no UI indicator when the user is offline. API calls will silently fail, and the user won't understand why features aren't working.
findings.impact: Users may repeatedly attempt actions that fail silently when offline, creating confusion and frustration.
findings.recommendation: Add an offline banner/toast that appears when navigator.onLine is false, and queue or gracefully degrade API-dependent features.

findings.title: AI responses not streamed — full wait for interpretation
findings.description: All AI interpretation calls (interpret, followup, patterns, compare, recap) wait for the complete response before displaying anything. For longer interpretations (Celtic Cross, pattern analysis), users stare at a spinner for 10-20 seconds with no feedback.
findings.impact: Poor perceived performance. Users may think the app is frozen during long AI calls. Streaming would show progressive text and feel much faster.
findings.recommendation: Implement streaming responses using Lambda response streaming or API Gateway WebSocket. On the frontend, use a streaming reader to progressively display interpretation text as it arrives.

findings.title: Card notes not utilized in AI interpretations
findings.description: Users can save personal notes on each card in the Library page (via saveCardNote API), but these notes are never fed into AI interpretation prompts. The personal meanings and associations a user builds over time could dramatically improve interpretation relevance.
findings.impact: Missed opportunity to personalize AI interpretations. Users invest time writing notes but get no return on that investment in readings.
findings.recommendation: When generating interpretations, fetch the user's card notes for the drawn cards and include them in the AI prompt as personal context. This makes interpretations more personally meaningful over time.

findings.title: Calendar/timeline view for reading history
findings.description: Users can only view readings as a reverse-chronological list. A calendar view showing which days had readings, daily cards, and journal entries would provide a powerful visual overview of their tarot practice over time.
findings.impact: Would significantly improve the 'personal journey' narrative. Calendar views drive engagement by making gaps visible and encouraging daily practice.
findings.recommendation: Add a calendar component to the history or recap page showing activity dots for each day. Clicking a day shows that day's readings, daily card, and journal entries.

findings.title: AI-powered daily affirmations based on card of the day
findings.description: The daily card page shows the card meaning and allows setting an intention, but doesn't generate a personalized AI affirmation based on the drawn card and the user's intention.
findings.impact: Would increase daily engagement and perceived value. Affirmations aligned with the day's card create a more personal experience.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'Generate Affirmation' button that sends the daily card + intention to a new AI endpoint. Display the affirmation prominently with option to copy or share.

findings.title: PDF/printable reading reports
findings.description: Users can copy readings as text and share via link, but there's no way to generate a beautifully formatted PDF of a reading. This is a common request in tarot apps and could be a premium feature.
findings.impact: Would add a tangible output users can keep, print, or share physically. Could be positioned as a premium feature for monetization.
findings.recommendation: Generate PDF reports using @react-pdf/renderer or server-side with Puppeteer. Include card symbols, positions, meanings, and interpretation.

findings.title: No push notification system for streak reminders
findings.description: The app tracks streaks (displayed on home page and settings) but has no way to remind users to maintain their streak. No push notifications, no email reminders. The service worker exists but only handles caching, not push notifications.
findings.impact: Users break streaks simply by forgetting. Push notifications are the standard retention mechanism for daily-habit apps.
findings.recommendation: Implement web push notifications via the service worker for daily card reminders and streak-at-risk alerts. Add notification preferences in settings. Use the Web Push API with VAPID keys.

findings.title: Unused _deletedReading state pattern in history page
findings.description: The history page implements an undo-delete pattern with a _deletedReading state variable. The underscore prefix convention typically means 'intentionally unused' but this variable is actively used in the undo flow.
findings.impact: Minor code clarity issue that may confuse contributors.
findings.recommendation: Rename _deletedReading to deletedReading or pendingDelete to better reflect its active use.

findings.title: PROJECT-LOG.md is outdated and inaccurate
findings.description: PROJECT-LOG.md contains inaccurate information: states 'Not a git repo' (it is), lists features as incomplete that are now fully implemented.
findings.impact: Misleads contributors about project status.
findings.recommendation: Either update PROJECT-LOG.md to accurately reflect current state or remove it in favor of the README.md which is more current.

findings.title: Community/social features for shared tarot practice
findings.description: The app is entirely single-player. The share feature creates a read-only public link but there's no way for users to interact with each other — no comments on shared readings, no group spreads, no community feed, no tarot circles.
findings.impact: Community features are the #1 growth driver for spiritual/wellness apps. They create network effects, increase retention, and enable organic discovery.
findings.recommendation: Start small: allow comments on shared readings, add a public community feed of voluntarily-shared readings. Later: tarot circles (groups), collaborative readings.
